explanation_md: `math::triple(7)` calls the `triple` function inside the `math` namespace, returning `7 * 3 = 21`. The `math::` prefix is required because the function is scoped to that namespace.

prompt_md: Which of these correctly describes the one-definition rule as it applies to functions across multiple files?

options.a: A function may be defined in multiple `.cpp` files as long as the definitions are identical
options.b: A function may be declared many times but may be defined only once across the entire program
options.c: A function must be both declared and defined in the same file
options.d: A function body in a header file is fine as long as the header has a guard

explanation_md: The one-definition rule (ODR) says a function may be defined exactly once in the whole linked program — regardless of how many `.cpp` files exist. It may be declared (forward-declared) in as many places as needed; declarations do not count as definitions. Putting a function body in a header violates the ODR when two `.cpp` files include that header.
`half(10)` returns `10 / 2 = 5`. `half(7)` returns `7 / 2 = 3` — integer division truncates toward zero. Both results are printed on separate lines.

options.a: The forward declaration of `area` is wrong — it should return `void`
options.b: `area` uses `+` instead of `*`, producing the sum instead of the product
options.c: `printResult` calls `area` before it is defined — linker error
options.d: `main` should call `area` directly instead of going through `printResult`

explanation_md: The `area` function returns `w + h` (sum) when it should return `w * h` (product). For input `3 4`, the sum is `7` but the expected area is `12`. The forward declaration, call chain, and function signatures are all correct — the only bug is the wrong arithmetic operator.

prompt_md:
A header file contains:

```cpp
#ifndef TOOLS_H
#define TOOLS_H
int double_it(int x);
#endif
```

Two `.cpp` files both include this header. What happens during compilation and linking?

options.a: Compile error: the guard prevents the second file from seeing the declaration
options.b: Linker error: two definitions of `double_it` are seen
options.c: Both files compile successfully; the linker sees one definition if `double_it` is defined in exactly one `.cpp` file
options.d: The guard only works within a single file, so both files get duplicate declarations

explanation_md: Each `.cpp` file is compiled independently. The guard fires within each individual compilation unit, preventing double-inclusion in that unit. Across two separate `.cpp` files it is perfectly fine for both to see the declaration — declarations are not definitions. As long as the actual function body appears in exactly one `.cpp`, the linker finds exactly one definition and links successfully.
